6778d33d2c73527d87131db6.question: Diniarchus is revealed to be the father of a child at the end of a play concerning a character with this profession, who swindles the title character Truculentus. In another play, the slave Milphiddipa (Mill-Fiddip-uh) serves a character with this profession named Acroteletium (Ah-cro-tel-uh-TEE-um) who assists Palaestrio (pah-lah-EE-stree-oh) in reuniting Philocomasium (*) (Phil-oh-coh-may-zium) and Pleusicles (Plu-SIC-leez). In the play Poenulus (Po-EN-you-less), Hanno proves the identity of two characters with this profession, allowing Adelphasium to marry Agorastocles (Ah-gore-ast-oh-cleez). Roman plays featured silent types of characters with this profession called scortum, as opposed to a meretrix. Plautus created characters with, for 10 points, what profession, including Erotia in Menaechmi (Men-ACH-mi)?
6778d33d2c73527d87131db6.question_sanitized: Diniarchus is revealed to be the father of a child at the end of a play concerning a character with this profession, who swindles the title character Truculentus. In another play, the slave Milphiddipa (Mill-Fiddip-uh) serves a character with this profession named Acroteletium (Ah-cro-tel-uh-TEE-um) who assists Palaestrio (pah-lah-EE-stree-oh) in reuniting Philocomasium (*) (Phil-oh-coh-may-zium) and Pleusicles (Plu-SIC-leez). In the play Poenulus (Po-EN-you-less), Hanno proves the identity of two characters with this profession, allowing Adelphasium to marry Agorastocles (Ah-gore-ast-oh-cleez). Roman plays featured silent types of characters with this profession called scortum, as opposed to a meretrix. Plautus created characters with, for 10 points, what profession, including Erotia in Menaechmi (Men-ACH-mi)?
6778d33d2c73527d87131db6.answer: prostitutes [or hetaira, or courtesans, or sex workers; accept meretrix or scortum before mentioned]
6778d33d2c73527d87131db6.answer_sanitized: prostitutes [or hetaira, or courtesans, or sex workers; accept meretrix or scortum before mentioned]

6778d33d2c73527d87131dc1.question: This play’s chorus asks one character whether he and the Greeks in Troy took turns having sex with Helen. Characters in this play offer excuses like a sprained ankle and dust in their eyes while reneging on an earlier plan. Another character in this play boasts that he doesn’t sacrifice any of his food to deities other than his stomach after earlier inquiring about his (*) stolen food. That character christens one of his servants Ganymede before having sex with him in this play set after the Trojan war near Mount Etna. Odysseus helps Silenus escape the title character by blinding the latter in, for 10 points, what only completely surviving satyr play, written by Euripides about the title one-eyed creature?

That character christens one of his servants Ganymede before having sex with him in this play set after the Trojan war near Mount Etna. Odysseus helps Silenus escape the title character by blinding the latter in, for 10 points, what only completely surviving satyr play, written by Euripides about the title one-eyed creature?

6778d33e2c73527d87131dce.question: This man names a villa in Pompeii containing a fresco of him seated reading one of his works. In one of his plays, confusion over one character nursing a baby assumed to be hers threatens to derail the wedding between Plangon and Moschion (Mohss-kee-own). Saint Paul’s phrase “Bad company corrupts good character” as well as Caesar’s (*) “the die is cast” are said to be derived from this author, who wrote about Demeas and his lover Chrysis in The Girl from Samos. Pan narrates one of his plays in which he causes Sostratos to fall in love with the title character’s daughter before he saves that man, Knemon, from drowning in a well. For 10 points, identify this New Comedy author of Dyskolos.

In one of his plays, confusion over one character nursing a baby assumed to be hers threatens to derail the wedding between Plangon and Moschion (Mohss-kee-own). Saint Paul's phrase "Bad company corrupts good character" as well as Caesar's (*) "the die is cast" are said to be derived from this author, who wrote about Demeas and his lover Chrysis in The Girl from Samos. Pan narrates one of his plays in which he causes Sostratos to fall in love with the title character's daughter before he saves that man, Knemon, from drowning in a well. For 10 points, identify this New Comedy author of Dyskolos.

6778d33e2c73527d87131df1.question: James Ker analyzed three different representations of this event including a Luca Giordano painting of it in a 2009 book. Cassius Dio mocks the central figure of this event by describing a failed attempt to kill his wife during it. Natalis’ testimony instigated this event which Granius Silvanus oversaw. This event’s central figure offered “imago vitae (*) suae” or “the image of his life” and eventually a libation to Jupiter the Liberator and while being assisted into a bathtub. Pompeia Paulina attempted to kill herself out of grief during, for 10 points, what event described by Tacitus in which Nero ordered the death of his Stoic tutor, a member of the Pisonian conspiracy?
6778d33e2c73527d87131df1.question_sanitized: James Ker analyzed three different representations of this event including a Luca Giordano painting of it in a 2009 book. Cassius Dio mocks the central figure of this event by describing a failed attempt to kill his wife during it. Natalis' testimony instigated this event which Granius Silvanus oversaw. This event's central figure offered "imago vitae (*) suae" or "the image of his life" and eventually a libation to Jupiter the Liberator and while being assisted into a bathtub. Pompeia Paulina attempted to kill herself out of grief during, for 10 points, what event described by Tacitus in which Nero ordered the death of his Stoic tutor, a member of the Pisonian conspiracy?
6778d33e2c73527d87131df1.answer: death of Seneca the Younger [or Lucius Annaeus Seneca; accept suicide for death]
6778d33e2c73527d87131df1.answer_sanitized: death of Seneca the Younger [or Lucius Annaeus Seneca; accept suicide for death]

6778d33e2c73527d87131df8.question: The Venetian samite originally relied on importing this good from the Byzantines. Pamphile of Kos discovered a new technique for this resource, which Pliny claims is made from “lanificia”. Glass was commonly traded for this resource. Ptolemy first referenced the Stone Tower, which Maes Titianus may have first reached in his journey to acquire this good. Lana pinna may have confusingly been called the sea (*) form of this resource. Tacitus relates how the senate under Tiberius banned the use of this material, which Seneca defended, explaining why women should not rely on it. Pomponius Mela described a land named for this material forming the central coast line along an eastern ocean. Romans thought trees produced, for 10 points, what material which they traveled to China to obtain?
6778d33e2c73527d87131df8.question_sanitized: The Venetian samite originally relied on importing this good from the Byzantines. Pamphile of Kos discovered a new technique for this resource, which Pliny claims is made from "lanificia". Glass was commonly traded for this resource. Ptolemy first referenced the Stone Tower, which Maes Titianus may have first reached in his journey to acquire this good. Lana pinna may have confusingly been called the sea (*) form of this resource. Tacitus relates how the senate under Tiberius banned the use of this material, which Seneca defended, explaining why women should not rely on it. Pomponius Mela described a land named for this material forming the central coast line along an eastern ocean. Romans thought trees produced, for 10 points, what material which they traveled to China to obtain?
6778d33e2c73527d87131df8.answer: silk [accept silkworms; accept sea silk before mentioned; accept sericum; accept bombycina or bombyx. If players are curious, Seneca thought silk was too revealing and not modest enough for women.]
6778d33e2c73527d87131df8.answer_sanitized: silk [accept silkworms; accept sea silk before mentioned; accept sericum; accept bombycina or bombyx. If players are curious, Seneca thought silk was too revealing and not modest enough for women.]

6778d33f2c73527d87131dfc.question: This character asks Demeter to “make me worthy of thy mystic rites!” before another character chooses to pray to his own “newly minted” gods. This character accuses another character of arguing that “life is not life” with his depiction of women giving birth in temples and having sex with their brothers. The poetic device lekythion was coined by this character as he uses the phrase “lost his little (*) oil flask” to interrupt another character whom he challenged to recite prologues from the latter’s plays. Sophocles is given this man’s chair in Hades after he defeats Euripides in a literary contest in Aristophanes’ play The Frogs. For 10 points, identify this real-life playwright of the Oresteia.
6778d33f2c73527d87131dfc.question_sanitized: This character asks Demeter to "make me worthy of thy mystic rites!" before another character chooses to pray to his own "newly minted" gods. The poetic device lekythion was coined by this character as he uses the phrase "lost his little (*) oil flask" to interrupt another character whom he challenged to recite prologues from the latter's plays. Sophocles is given this man's chair in Hades after he defeats Euripides in a literary contest in Aristophanes' play The Frogs. For 10 points, identify this real-life playwright of the Oresteia.

6778d33f2c73527d87131e0a.question: Gnaeus Flavius studied the law as a scribe for this man before publicizing legal procedures to make them accessible to plebs. This politician argued that his term limit did not apply to him when he refused to step down with Gaius Plautus Venox; he used his “extended” term to complete construction on an aqueduct. This man stated “Every man is the architect (*) of his own fortune” while urging Rome to reject peace terms from Pyrrhus of Epirus. Livy criticized this man for writing a list of senators that included freedmen. Rome was connected to Brundisium by a road that this politician commissioned, along which Spartacus and other rebellious slaves were crucified. The first Roman aqueduct was named after, for 10 points, what blind 4th century BC censor who names a famous road?
6778d33f2c73527d87131e0a.question_sanitized: Gnaeus Flavius studied the law as a scribe for this man before publicizing legal procedures to make them accessible to plebs. This politician argued that his term limit did not apply to him when he refused to step down with Gaius Plautus Venox; he used his "extended" term to complete construction on an aqueduct. This man stated "Every man is the architect (*) of his own fortune" while urging Rome to reject peace terms from Pyrrhus of Epirus. Livy criticized this man for writing a list of senators that included freedmen. Rome was connected to Brundisium by a road that this politician commissioned, along which Spartacus and other rebellious slaves were crucified. The first Roman aqueduct was named after, for 10 points, what blind 4th century BC censor who names a famous road?
6778d33f2c73527d87131e0a.answer: Appius Claudius Crassus Caecus [or Claudius Caecus or Appius Claudius Caecus; prompt Appius or Claudius alone]
6778d33f2c73527d87131e0a.answer_sanitized: Appius Claudius Crassus Caecus [or Claudius Caecus or Appius Claudius Caecus; prompt Appius or Claudius alone]

6778d33f2c73527d87131e18.question: Prior to this event, its central participant writes a letter asking its recipient “Who will give over their fields to be controlled by nobodies?” and “What crime have I committed except to have loved?”. The sister of this event’s central participant is told to reside among the nymphs of Numicius after she recounts this event in book 8 of a poem by Silius Italicus. The central participant follows the advice of a Massylian (*) priestess to burn clothes after interpreting omens including water turning black and wine turning into blood. The death of Hasdrubal the Boetharch may have inspired the use of a funeral pyre in, for 10 points, what event taking place after Aeneas leaves Carthage?

The death of Hasdrubal the Boetharch may have inspired the use of a funeral pyre in, for 10 points, what event taking place after Aeneas leaves Carthage?
6778d33f2c73527d87131e18.answer: suicide of Dido [accept death for “suicide”; accept Elissa for “Dido”; the first clue is from Ovid’s Heroides]

6778d33f2c73527d87131e22.question: A group of these people travel to Delphi to serve Apollo Loxias after he saved their city in a play named for them. Aeschylus’ play The Persians was based on an earlier play by Phynichus named for these people who relate the events of the Battle of Salamis. Seneca the Younger’s shortest surviving play is likely inspired by a play named for these people in which (*) Menoeceus (men-oh-kay-us) commits suicide to save his city. Unlike the Seven Against Thebes, that play titled for these people condemns Eteocles for retaining the throne and depicts Jocasta committing suicide following her sons’ duel. Euripides wrote a play about the siege of Thebes with, for 10 points, what people serving as a chorus?

6778d33f2c73527d87131e22.answer: Phoenician women [prompt on partial answer; prompt on Tyrian for Phoenician by asking “What civilization founded Tyre?”]

6778d3402c73527d87131e2c.question: Pescennius Niger’s army was surprised when Septimus Severus did not travel through this location at their third and final battle. The traitor Quintus Labienus (lay-bee-en-us) and his Persian allies were defeated by Ventidius (ven-tid-ee-us) in a 39 BC battle at this location. St. Paul’s description of his home city as “no insignificant city” may have referred to his difficulty of traveling through this location. The Persian army anticipated destroying granaries near this location before Alexander the Great’s army could make it here prior to the Battle of Issus. Tarsus (*) is home to, for 10 points, what highly coveted pass in the Taurus mountains named for a region in southeastern Turkey?

6778d3402c73527d87131e2c.answer: Cilician Gates [accept Gülek Pass; accept Kilíkies pýles (Κιλίκιες πύλες); accept Pylae Ciliciae]
6778d3402c73527d87131e2c.answer_sanitized: Cilician Gates [accept Gulek Pass; accept Kilikies pyles (Κιλικιες πυλες); accept Pylae Ciliciae]

6778d3402c73527d87131e3b.question: John Dryden’s translation of this woman’s story is often read as comparing her to Mary II after the Glorious Revolution. The most famous poem of the 1st century BC writer Helvius Cinna concerned this woman, whose father is sometimes said to be the Assyrian king Theias. It’s not Phaedra, but this woman’s initial plan to hang herself is thwarted when her nurse finds her before promising to help her fulfill her lustful desire during the festival of Ceres (*) . She flees to the land of the Sabaeans where she gives birth to her son. For 10 points, name this woman who seduced her father Cinyras before turning into her namesake tree and giving birth to Adonis?

She flees to the land of the Sabaeans where she gives birth to her son. For 10 points, name this woman who seduced her father Cinyras before turning into her namesake tree and giving birth to Adonis?

6778d3402c73527d87131e53.question: This woman’s speech is compared to the sphinx in a highly allusive Hellenistic poem possibly by Lycophron that refers to her as Alexandra. This character first appears in a play carrying torches to honor Hymen and Hecate and sings a song to her mother about her wedding which “will destroy those whom thou and I most hate”. When asked “What can a victor fear?”, she replies “What he doth not fear” as the leader of the chorus in a Latin play which ends with another character ordering her death. Athena plots revenge against the Greeks (*) because of their failure to punish this character’s rapist at the beginning of Euripides Trojan Women. For 10 points, name this woman taken captive by Agamemnon after the Trojan war, who was raped by Ajax the Lesser in the temple of Athena.
